Party_Details.Name: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Facts: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd is the first plaintiff and a Singapore company owned by ABB Asea Brown Boveri Ltd, a Swiss company. It was set up in Singapore to be the holding company of the Singapore businesses of the ABB Group and is the sole shareholder of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and ABB Industry Pte Ltd. It does not itself undertake commercial activities but functions as a holding company within the ABB Singapore Group, which is part of a worldwide group of companies manufacturing, marketing and selling products for industrial and commercial uses, including low, medium and high voltage circuit breakers.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether, and to what extent, the defendant, as a senior manager and director within the ABB Singapore Group, owed contractual and fiduciary duties to the plaintiffs, and whether his conduct in relation to external entities, including XIHARI, Huadian, Great Vision, Xiamen Fu Cheng, Webmoney and related ventures, breached those duties.
Party_Details.Rule: Contractual terms in employment agreements and employee handbooks may be incorporated into an employee's contract if it is the contractual intention of the parties and the terms are apt to form part of the contract, as articulated in authorities such as Alexander v Standard Telephones & Cables Ltd (No 2) and Intergraph Systems South East Asia Pte Ltd v Zhang Yiguang. Directors and senior managerial employees may owe fiduciary duties of loyalty, good faith, avoidance of conflicts of interest, and duties not to misuse confidential information, as recognised in cases including Canadian Aero Service Ltd v O’Malley, Shepherds Investment Ltd v Walters, British Midland Tool v Midland International, and IDC Ltd v Cooley. An employee’s duty of fidelity includes not secretly engaging in activities that may harm the employer’s business, as discussed in Hivac Limited v Park Royal Scientific Instruments Limited.
Party_Details.Application: The court examined the employment contracts between the defendant and the ABB entities, including the express clauses and the reference to 'General Terms and Conditions of Employment of the Country Organisation', and considered evidence that the ABB Employee Handbook was made available to employees and treated in practice as governing staff terms and benefits. The judge assessed whether the Handbook’s provisions on conflicts of interest, protection of company assets, and prohibition of private participation in other business activities were incorporated into the defendant’s contracts. The court evaluated the defendant’s seniority, responsibilities, and participation in the Country Management Team to determine whether his position as president and director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and as general manager and vice-president in ABB Industry Pte Ltd placed him in a fiduciary position similar to that of a director. The judge considered the pleaded express and implied duties, including duties of good faith, fidelity, avoidance of conflicts, and protection of confidential and proprietary information, and compared them with the duties recognised in case law. The court then analysed the factual matrix surrounding the defendant’s interactions with XIHARI and Huadian, his communications with former ABB engineer Guenther Leonhardt, and his involvement in other companies, to assess whether these activities were inconsistent with his contractual and fiduciary obligations to the ABB Singapore entities.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The claim of ABB Holdings Pte Ltd against the defendant was dismissed.

Party_Details.Name: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Facts: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd is the second plaintiff and a Singapore company within the ABB Singapore Group. It manufactures miniature circuit breakers, which are low voltage products. Between January 1997 and January 2001, it was the defendant’s employer. During this period, the defendant held positions including President of the company and, from July 1998 to February 2003, director. The company’s business related to low voltage products in Singapore and South East Asia and it owned and ran manufacturing plants in Batam and Jakarta assembling miniature circuit breakers. Its business did not include manufacturing medium voltage products.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant, as president and director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, owed fiduciary and contractual duties to this company, and whether his conduct in assisting XIHARI’s search for a technical adviser and in preparing to join Huadian, a medium voltage switchgear manufacturer, breached those duties.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors owe fiduciary duties to their companies, including duties to act in good faith in the best interests of the company, to avoid placing themselves in positions of conflict between duty and personal interest, and to disclose to the company information of concern to it that is relevant for it to know. Senior officers acting in a managerial capacity may owe similar fiduciary obligations. These principles are reflected in authorities such as Canadian Aero Service Ltd v O’Malley, Gower’s Principles of Modern Company Law, Shepherds Investment Ltd v Walters, British Midland Tool v Midland International, and IDC Ltd v Cooley. Contractual terms from employee handbooks may be incorporated into employment contracts where the parties intend them to apply and the terms are apt to be contractual, as in Alexander v Standard Telephones & Cables Ltd (No 2) and Intergraph Systems South East Asia Pte Ltd v Zhang Yiguang.
Party_Details.Application: The court considered that the defendant, as a director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, occupied a fiduciary position vis-à-vis this company. It reviewed evidence that the company remained an operating entity during the relevant period, with manufacturing plants in Indonesia, and that the defendant’s directorial role continued until February 2003. The judge assessed whether the defendant’s knowledge of XIHARI’s plans to develop a new generation of medium voltage circuit breakers and to establish a manufacturing company, and his active role in facilitating contact between XIHARI and former ABB engineer Guenther Leonhardt, were matters of concern to the second plaintiff and relevant for it to know, given its position within the ABB Singapore Group and its involvement in low voltage products. The court examined whether the defendant’s actions in assisting a potential competitor’s technical development and in preparing business plans for Huadian while still holding office as director were consistent with his fiduciary obligations to avoid conflicts of interest and to act in the company’s best interests. The judge also evaluated whether the second plaintiff’s lack of direct involvement in medium voltage manufacturing limited the scope of any fiduciary breach, while recognising that information about potential competition in related product markets could still be material to the company.
Party_Details.Conclusion: There was judgment for ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd against the defendant for either damages to be assessed or an account of the defendant’s profits to be taken, at the election of this plaintiff.

Party_Details.Name: ABB Industry Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Facts: ABB Industry Pte Ltd is the third plaintiff and a Singapore company within the ABB Singapore Group. It carries on business as, among other things, a manufacturer of switchgears and an installer of electrical works. It is involved in low voltage products and also supplies medium voltage products as part of projects or packages. With effect from 1 January 2001, the defendant’s employment was transferred from ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd to ABB Industry Pte Ltd. Under this company, he held positions including General Manager, Business Area Low Voltage Products and Systems, and from September 2001, Vice-President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products, Automation Technology Products Division Asia South. He was part of the top management, attended Country Management Team meetings where various businesses, including medium voltage products, were discussed, and had authority to hire and fire employees. His employment was governed by successive contracts and an Employee Handbook containing policies on conflicts of interest, protection of company assets, and restrictions on private participation in other business activities.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant, as a very senior employee and vice-president of ABB Industry Pte Ltd, owed fiduciary and contractual duties extending beyond low voltage products to include matters concerning medium voltage products, and whether his assistance to XIHARI in engaging a technical adviser and his preparatory work for Huadian while still employed breached those duties.
Party_Details.Rule: Senior managerial employees who are part of a company’s top management and authorised to act on its behalf may owe fiduciary duties similar to those of directors, including duties to act in good faith in the best interests of the company, to avoid conflicts between personal interests and duties, to disclose to the company information of concern to it, and not to make improper use of information acquired by virtue of their position. These principles are drawn from authorities such as Canadian Aero Service Ltd v O’Malley, Gower’s Principles of Modern Company Law, and Shepherds Investment Ltd v Walters. An employee’s duty of fidelity prohibits secretly engaging in activities that may harm the employer’s business, as in Hivac Limited v Park Royal Scientific Instruments Limited. Contractual terms from an employee handbook, including clauses on conflicts of interest, protection of confidential and proprietary information, and prohibitions on private participation in other business activities, may be incorporated into the employment contract where intended by the parties, following principles in Alexander v Standard Telephones & Cables Ltd (No 2) and Intergraph Systems South East Asia Pte Ltd v Zhang Yiguang. A director’s duty to promote the company’s best interests includes a duty to inform the company of activities that may damage those interests, as discussed in British Midland Tool v Midland International and IDC Ltd v Cooley.
Party_Details.Application: The court found that the Employee Handbook, including clauses on avoiding conflicts of interest, protecting company assets and confidential information, and prohibiting private participation in other business activities, was intended to form part of the defendant’s employment terms with ABB Industry Pte Ltd. The judge considered the defendant’s senior status, his role in general management and business development, his participation in Country Management Team meetings, and his authority over staff, and concluded that he was part of the top management and owed fiduciary duties to ABB Industry Pte Ltd. The court determined that these fiduciary duties were not confined to low voltage products but extended to matters concerning medium voltage products, given the company’s involvement in supplying such products for projects and the defendant’s exposure to information about that business. The judge examined the defendant’s knowledge that XIHARI intended to develop and market a new generation of medium voltage circuit breakers and to establish a manufacturing company, and his active role in communicating with former ABB engineer Guenther Leonhardt to secure his services as technical adviser and technical controller for XIHARI and the new manufacturing entity. The court reasoned that this information was of concern to ABB Industry Pte Ltd and relevant for it to know, as the planned products had the potential to compete in markets where ABB Industry Pte Ltd supplied medium voltage products. By failing to inform ABB Industry Pte Ltd of XIHARI’s plans and by assisting XIHARI to obtain expert technical support, the defendant acted inconsistently with his duties to act in the company’s best interests and to avoid conflicts of interest. The judge also assessed evidence that, after deciding to join Huadian, the defendant spent time while still employed by ABB Industry Pte Ltd in preparing business plans and forecasts for Huadian, as reflected in his presentation at Huadian’s first board meeting on 3 March 2003. This preparatory work for a prospective competitor, undertaken before his resignation, was evaluated against his contractual obligation not to participate in other business activities and his duty of fidelity to devote his efforts to his employer’s business.
Party_Details.Conclusion: There was judgment for ABB Industry Pte Ltd against the defendant for either damages to be assessed or an account of the defendant’s profits to be taken, at the election of this plaintiff.

Party_Details.Name: Sher Hock Guan Charles
Party_Details.Facts: Sher Hock Guan Charles is the defendant, a Singaporean who worked for various companies in the ABB Group between 1990 and February 2003. He worked in Xiamen, China for ABB China Ltd and ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd before being based in Singapore from January 1997. Between January 1997 and January 2001, he was employed by ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, holding positions including President and, from July 1998 to February 2003, director. From 1 January 2001, his employment was transferred to ABB Industry Pte Ltd, where he served as General Manager, Business Area Low Voltage Products and Systems, and from September 2001 as Vice-President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products, Automation Technology Products Division Asia South. His employment was governed by three successive contracts and an Employee Handbook. After leaving ABB Industry Pte Ltd in February 2003, he became General Manager of Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd, a Chinese company manufacturing switchgears and circuit breakers, with effect from 3 March 2003, and later became its Managing Director after a German company, Gelpag mbH, in which he was a shareholder, acquired shares in Huadian. During and after his employment with the ABB Singapore entities, he had connections with other companies, including Great Vision International Pte Ltd, Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd, Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd, Gelpag International Pte Ltd, and Gelpag mbH. The plaintiffs alleged that, by reason of his positions and employment, he owed them express, implied, and fiduciary duties and that he breached these duties through his involvement with XIHARI, Huadian, and other ventures. He denied many of the alleged duties and breaches, contending that his contractual obligations were limited to the express terms of his employment agreements and that he did not misuse confidential information or improperly compete with the plaintiffs during his employment.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant, in his capacities as director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and senior manager of ABB Industry Pte Ltd, owed contractual and fiduciary duties to the plaintiffs, and whether his conduct in assisting XIHARI to secure a technical adviser, in preparing to join and manage Huadian, and in participating in or facilitating other business ventures while still employed, constituted breaches of those duties and gave rise to liability.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors and senior managerial employees owe fiduciary duties to act in good faith in the best interests of their companies, to avoid placing themselves in positions where their personal interests may conflict with their duties, to disclose to their companies information that is of concern and relevant for the companies to know, and not to make improper use of information acquired by virtue of their positions to gain an advantage for themselves or others or to cause detriment to the company. These principles are reflected in Canadian Aero Service Ltd v O’Malley, Gower’s Principles of Modern Company Law, Shepherds Investment Ltd v Walters, British Midland Tool v Midland International, and IDC Ltd v Cooley. Employees owe duties of fidelity not to misuse confidential information or secretly engage in activities that may harm their employer’s business, as in Hivac Limited v Park Royal Scientific Instruments Limited. Contractual terms, including those in employee handbooks, may be incorporated into employment contracts where the parties intend them to apply and the terms are apt to form part of the contract, following Alexander v Standard Telephones & Cables Ltd (No 2) and Intergraph Systems South East Asia Pte Ltd v Zhang Yiguang. The scope of any implied or fiduciary duty is determined by the nature of the employee’s role, responsibilities, and the business context.
Party_Details.Application: The court analysed the defendant’s three employment contracts and the ABB Employee Handbook to determine the express and implied contractual duties he owed. It concluded that the Handbook’s provisions on conflicts of interest, protection of company assets and confidential information, and prohibition of private participation in other business activities were incorporated into his contracts with the ABB Singapore entities. The judge rejected the plaintiffs’ contention that a continuing contractual obligation to promote the interests of the entire ABB Group persisted beyond the first contract, noting that later contracts and the Handbook did not contain such a term. The court then considered whether the defendant’s senior roles as president and director of ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and as general manager and vice-president of ABB Industry Pte Ltd placed him in a fiduciary position. It found that, as a director of the second plaintiff, he clearly owed fiduciary duties, and that his top management status in the third plaintiff, including his authority to hire and fire and his participation in Country Management Team meetings, meant he owed fiduciary duties to ABB Industry Pte Ltd similar to those of a director. The judge examined the factual allegations concerning his involvement with Great Vision and Xiamen Fu Cheng, including the property development project in Xiamen and the RMB70,000 travel expenses entry, and concluded that the evidence did not establish active participation in those businesses during his employment, beyond passing on a property opportunity to his brother. The court assessed his role in incorporating Webmoney and holding a nominal share, finding that the company remained dormant and that his limited involvement did not amount to participation in another business in breach of his duties. The judge then focused on his interactions with XIHARI and Huadian. Evidence showed that, while still employed by ABB Industry Pte Ltd, the defendant knew that XIHARI intended to develop and market a new generation of medium voltage circuit breakers and to establish a manufacturing company, and that he actively communicated with former ABB engineer Guenther Leonhardt to secure his services as technical adviser and technical controller for XIHARI and the new company. The court reasoned that this information was of concern to the second and third plaintiffs and relevant for them to know, given their roles in the ABB Singapore Group and involvement in low and medium voltage products. By failing to inform them of XIHARI’s plans and by assisting XIHARI to obtain expert technical support, the defendant acted inconsistently with his fiduciary duties and his duty of fidelity. The judge also evaluated evidence that, after deciding to join Huadian, the defendant spent time while still employed by ABB Industry Pte Ltd preparing business plans and forecasts for Huadian, as reflected in his presentation at Huadian’s first board meeting on 3 March 2003. The court inferred that this preparatory work was undertaken before his resignation and constituted work for a prospective competitor during his employment, contrary to his contractual obligation not to participate in other business activities and his duty to devote his efforts to his employer’s business. At the same time, the court found no evidence that the defendant had supplied confidential technical information about ABB’s medium voltage products to XIHARI, Huadian, or related entities, and it did not attribute the technical development of Huadian’s products to him.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The defendant was held liable to ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd and ABB Industry Pte Ltd, with judgment entered for those plaintiffs for either damages to be assessed or an account of his profits to be taken at their election, while the claim by ABB Holdings Pte Ltd against him was dismissed.
